nameMeanings.name: Hart
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English and North German: nickname from Middle English hert (Old English heorot), Middle Low German hërte, harte ‘hart, stag’, perhaps for a quick-footed or timorous individual.
German: variant of   Hardt   1 and 2. It is also found in France (Alsace and Lorraine).
Jewish (Ashkenazic): artificial name or nickname from German and Yiddish hart ‘hard’.

variants.name: Hartmann
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Hans, Kurt, Otto, Erwin, Heinz, Helmut, Horst, Klaus, Manfred, Dieter, Dietrich, Franz.
North German and Danish: variant of   Hart   1.

German and Swiss German: from an ancient Germanic personal name composed of the elements hard ‘hard, strong’ + man ‘man’. This surname is also found in the Netherlands, France (mainly Alsace and Lorraine), Hungary, Czechia, and some other European countries; see also below. Compare Hartman .

variants.name: Francoeur
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Armand, Jacques, Aime, Marcel, Normand, Alcide, Amie, Anselme, Cecile, Clemence, Dominique, Georges.
French:
soldier's name from franc coeur ‘sincere heart’.

variants.name: Hartin
meanings: Irish (Donegal): Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó hArtáin ‘descendant of Artán’, a personal name formed from a diminutive of Art, a byname meaning ‘bear’ or ‘hero’. Compare Hart 8.
English: habitational name from Harting (Sussex), from the Old English personal name Heort + the Old English groupname suffix -ingas.

variants.name: Hartel
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Erwin, Erna, Gunter, Kurt, Lutz, Otto.
German (also Härtel): from a pet form of an ancient Germanic compound personal name based on the element hard ‘hardy, brave, strong’.
French (Normandy): occupational name, a derivative of   Hart   7.

variants.name: De Hart
meanings: Dutch: from Middle Dutch hart, modern Dutch hert ‘deer, hart’ or hart ‘heart’ (see Hart 5), with the definite article de ‘the’.

variants.name: Hardt
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Otto, Ernst, Erwin, Frieda, Gerhard, Gottlieb, Gunther, Hans, Heinz, Reinhold, Udo.
German: topographic name for someone who lived by woods used as pasture, from Middle High German hart.
German: from a short form of an ancient Germanic compound personal name beginning with hard ‘hardy, brave, strong’.

variants.name: Harten
meanings: North German: patronymic from   Hart   1.
Dutch: patronymic from   Hart   4.
Dutch (Van Harten): habitational name from a place so named near Renkum, Gelderland.

variants.name: Hartt
meanings: English (Norfolk): variant of   Hart  .

variants.name: Harte
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Brendan, Sinead.
English and North German: variant of   Hart   1.
Dutch: variant of   Hart   4.

variants.name: Hartjes
meanings: Dutch: patronymic from a pet form of   Hart   4.

nameMeanings.name: Mrs.
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: from Latin Ursus ‘bear’.

nameMeanings.name: Robert
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the many French names of Germanic origin that were introduced into Britain by the Normans; it has since remained in continuous use. It is derived from the nearly synonymous elements hrōd ‘fame’ + berht ‘bright, famous’, and had a native Old English predecessor of similar form (Hreodbeorht), which was supplanted by the Norman name. Two dukes of Normandy in the 11th century bore the name: the father of William the Conqueror (sometimes identified with the legendary Robert the Devil), and his eldest son. It was borne also by three kings of Scotland, notably Robert the Bruce ( 1274–1329 ), who freed Scotland from English domination. The altered short form Bob is very common, but Hob and Dob, which were common in the Middle Ages and gave rise to surnames, are extinct. See also Rupert .
